category.text: $cat2$/SERIOUS ASSAULTS/Serious Assaults DDC/Serious Assault DDC CL

questiontext.text:
One Sunday afternoon Isaiah was out biking on his trek mountain bike. He was looking for a car he could boost so that he could drive to his uncle’s party in Northland. As he rode down the road, he noticed a YMCA Fitness and Swim Centre up ahead. Isaiah knew that there was a large car park attached to the centre, and thought to himself that he may find an easy target car to steal in the car park.

As he slowly rode around the car park, he noticed an elderly lady called Charity come out of the front door of the rec centre and unlock her Toyota Aqua. She opened the boot of her car and put her gym bag and a flutter board in the boot. As he watched Charity, Isaiah thought to himself, “This is an easy target”.

When he was about 20 metres away from Charity and her Aqua, Isaiah stopped riding and laid his bike down on the ground. “Now to get her over here” Isaiah thought to himself. He walked towards Charity while calling out to her “Hey lady! Could you come and help me with my bike? The chain has come off.”

Charity walked toward Isaiah and said to him “I don’t know much about bikes I’m sorry”. 

“It doesn’t matter,” Isaiah replied, “If you hold the bike for me, I can put it back on.” 

Charity reached the bike and Isaiah picked it up for her to hold upright. Once Charity was holding the bike, Isaiah took his opportunity to leave her holding the bike while he sprinted over to the Aqua.

He reached the Aqua's driver's door, flung it open and jumped into the driver’s seat. Pulling a screwdriver out of his pocket he jammed it into the ignition barrel and started the car.

But before Isaiah had time to close the door and drive away, Charity had dumped the bike back on the ground and run over to her car. Charity was angry and determined to “stop this guy from stealing her car.” 

When she reached the still open driver’s door of her car, she grabbed hold of the top of it to stop Isaiah shutting it and driving off. 

Isaiah yelled “Let go lady! Its mine now.” He slammed the door trapping Charity’s fingers behind it. Isaiah realised Charity was trapped but he was desperate to get away, so he planted his foot and accelerated.

Charity, her fingers trapped in the car door, was dragged along with the car for several metres stumbling as she tried desperately to keep up with the accelerating Aqua. As Isaiah sped up Charity could no longer stay on her feet and fell. Her ring finger and her little finger were severed as Isaiah drove away.

A member of the public who witnessed the confrontation, called an ambulance. Charity was taken to hospital where her amputated fingers were unable to be re-attached as they were still in the vehicle. She required surgery to a seriously damaged middle finger and was expected to have nerve damage in her fingers and hand.

 In relation to any serious assault offence, discuss Isiah’s liability and the evidential sufficiency of a prosecution against him.

Sanjay is a security guard, whose main role as a security patrol officer, involved checking alarm activations, and the regular monitoring and checking of premises. One night he is advised by his company’s comms centre of multiple alarm activations at Eddie’s Bar and Bistro.

Sanjay drove to the location and around the carparking area of the building Eddie’s Bar was located in. Seeing nothing out of the ordinary, he decided to do a full external inspection of Eddie’s. He parked his security van, grabbed his torch and began his inspection.

Walking about halfway round the property Sanjay came to a side door of Eddies, and discovered  that it was wide open. Sanjay immediately radioed the comms centre of a confirmed break, and continued his external inspection, walking past the open door. 

The final quarter of the inspection involved a fenced courtyard to the bar, and Sanjay used his provided key to open the gate and inspect the patio-area. The fenced patio area was dotted with picnic tables and planter boxes with small trees.

As he passed into the fenced area, Sanjay noticed that the French door from the internal bar out to the patio area was also open. Sanjay walked up to the open patio door and shone his torch inside in an effort to see if any damage had been done inside the bistro.

Beside the patio door was a large planter box with a small tree with thick foliage. As Sanjay shone the beam of his torch through the open door, a male popped up from behind the tree foliage, giving Sanjay a fright. Sanjay immediately jumped backward into the patio space and again radioed comms that there was a suspect on site.

Sanjay was positioned between the male and the open fence gate. He slowly backed away from the male towards the gate, shining his torch in the eyes of the male. It was then that Sanjay noticed that the male was holding a knife in his hand, as the long silver blade glinted in the torch light.

The male lunged at Sanjay, who had no alternative but to drop his torch and wrap his arms around the male and take him to the ground lying on top of him. On the ground Sanjay tried to put all his body weight onto the male’s arm, so that he had no opportunity to stab Sanjay.

As he tried to hold the male by his body weight, the male struggled to get free. After thirty seconds of struggling on the ground the male managed to get his hand free, and Sanjay felt something hit the top of his head. This caused Sanjay to put his hands to his head in pain and lose any remaining grip he had of the male. The male pushed Sanjay off him, ran out through the open gate and down the road.

Sanjay lay on the ground until the police and ambulance arrived. After assessment by paramedics, Sanjay was diagnosed with a lump on his head with a small graze and small amount of dried blood. A Dog Unit tracked the male down the road, to a park, where he was arrested. When searched, a small crowbar was found in his pocket. (This is here to aid with decision-making in assault/injure/wound)

In relation to a serious assault offence, discuss the male’s liability and the evidential sufficiency of a prosecution against him.

A loud bang woke Chris early one Sunday morning. As he got out of bed to investigate, he heard another loud noise the sound of glass shattering which he believed came from outside his property. Chris went into another bedroom with a good view of the street and looked out the window. Seeing a neighbour standing in the middle of the road, he opened the window and called out, “What’s going on?” The neighbour replied, “There was a man who kicked over the rubbish bins and then started walking up your driveway. I challenged him, but he kept heading onto your property.”

Chris decided to go downstairs and check the back of his property through the kitchen window. As he entered the kitchen, he saw that the French doors leading to the patio were smashed, and two full-length windowpanes were shattered across the floor. Realising this was the source of the noise he’d heard earlier, Chris turned to head down the hall and out the front door to join his neighbour.

Before he could turn around, Chris was struck on the back of the head and immediately collapsed face-first onto the floor, losing consciousness. About thirty seconds later, he regained consciousness. A broken wine bottle lay next to him, and blood streamed down his back from a head wound. Though dizzy, Chris managed to stand, leaning on the kitchen bench for support. It was then that he saw a large male standing in his kitchen, holding another full bottle of wine.

Chris also realised that his neighbour had entered the house while he was unconscious and was now standing in the hallway with a golf club raised above his head. The neighbour was shouting at the intruder: “Get out now! The police are on the way. Just leave now!”

Without responding, the intruder launched himself at Chris. He kicked Chris’s leg out from under him, and Chris heard a crack. At the same time, the intruder brought the wine bottle down again—this time on the side of Chris’s head. The bottle shattered, and Chris lost consciousness, collapsing to the floor with his leg twisted at a right angle to his body.

Witnessing the attack, the neighbour struck the intruder across the back with the golf club, using as much force as he could muster. The intruder, still holding the neck of the broken wine bottle, lunged at the neighbour, slashing the side of his neck and ear. The neighbour screamed, “Get out!” and swung the golf club again, aiming for the intruder’s shoulder. The intruder dodged the swing and ran past him, shoving him roughly into the wall before fleeing down the hall and out the front door.

When police and ambulance arrived, they found Chris unconscious and not breathing on the floor. He was successfully defibrillated and transported to hospital in critical condition. He was diagnosed with several deep lacerations, a fractured skull, a brain bleed, extensive fractures to his facial bones, and four severe fractures to his leg—all requiring multiple surgeries over several months

In relation to a serious assault offence, discuss the Intruder’s liability and the evidential sufficiency of a prosecution against him.

Vinny went out for dinner with friends one evening. When he returned home, he parked his car in the carport as usual. As he drove in, he noticed his flatmate’s car parked on the road outside their flat and saw three people standing near it.

Because it was dark, Vinny walked down the driveway to check what was happening. When he reached the end of the driveway, he saw three males trying to break the front passenger window of his flatmate’s car using a rock.

One of the males saw Vinny watching and immediately walked toward him, followed closely by another male. Vinny turned to run, but the first male grabbed him from behind, pinning his arms. The second male stood in front of Vinny.

Fearing he was about to be punched, Vinny kicked the second male in the stomach, winding him. 

The male bent over clutching his stomach. 

The third male then approached, saw his friend in pain, and yelled: “What have you done to him?” He pulled a knife from his pocket and stabbed Vinny in the thigh, then pulled the knife out. Vinny screamed and fell to the ground, breaking free from the first male’s grip.

The first male helped the one Vinny had kicked, and together they ran off down the road, with the third male following.

Vinny’s flatmate heard him scream and found him lying on the footpath clutching his leg. Police were called, and Vinny was taken to hospital where his wound was cleaned and stitched.

In relation to a serious assault offence, discuss the criminal liability for the third male and whether there is sufficient evidence to prosecute him

One evening Tawa decided to go to the supermarket as his fridge was empty. He picked up his cousin Xavier on the way as he knew Xavier’s car was in the shop getting fixed. Together the two did their grocery shopping. Tawa finished his shopping earlier than Xavier and pushed his trolley to his car and began loading up his boot with several bags of his shopping.

While he was doing this a male approached him, waving as he walked towards Tawa’s car. Tawa looked up at the male and didn’t recognise him. “Hey bro, you got any spare cash?” the male said to him. “Nah mate!” said Tawa, pointing to crossbody pouch he was wearing. “Sorry I’ve only got cards on me.” The male didn’t say anything but turned and walked away.

A few minutes later Xavier arrived with his trolley and loaded his grocery bags into the back seat. He decided to sit in the backseat for the ride home and eat a pie that was in in one of his grocery bags. Tawa backed out of his carpark and drove slowly toward the carpark exit.

Suddenly the front passenger door opened and the same man who had approached Tawa for cash was standing at the open door. He yelled at Tawa “are you slaying (selling) drugs?” “No!” Tawa replied, “get out of my car”.

“Who are you slaying (selling) drugs for?” the man yelled. “No one mate” says Xavier from the back seat, “we both work.”

The male grabbed Tawa’s crossbody bag from the front passenger seat and started going through the contents of Tawa’s bag. “Fucken put that down” Tawa told the male. “Put it down or we’ll get out and put you down” he continued pointing to Xavier. “Yeah mate – don’t piss us off. Fuck off.” Xavier said.

The male stopped looking through the bag and reached behind him. He drew out a pistol from the back of his pants, pointed it towards Tawa’s face, and then moved it in an arc towards Xavier. “Still wanna have a go then?” he said. He then stepped backward still holding the pistol in one hand and Tawa’s bag in the other. He slammed the front passenger door closed, and Tawa and Xavier watched him run towards the exit of the car park, holding Tawa’s bag and putting the pistol in the back of his pants as he ran.

In relation to a serious assault offence, discuss the male’s liability and the evidential sufficiency of a prosecution against him.
